profile.behavior: Diurnal, social omnivore that commonly forages on the ground; extended family groups may cooperate in rearing young, and large communal roosts can form outside the breeding season.
field_marks: Large, all-black corvid with a robust, moderately sized black bill.
Adult plumage is glossy black, often showing a subtle blue-violet sheen in good light.
Dark eyes, black legs, and strong black feet.
Broad wings show five conspicuous, fingered primary tips in flight.
Tail is short and square-tipped to slightly rounded, not wedge-shaped.
Usually flies with steady, methodical wingbeats.
profile.habitat: Adaptable in open or semi-open places with trees and reliable food, including woodlands, forest edges, fields, farmland, shorelines, wetlands, and human-modified green spaces; generally avoids extensive unbroken forest and deserts.

sources.title: American Crow Overview — Cornell Lab of Ornithology
American Crow Life History — Cornell Lab of Ornithology

findings: Verified identity: American Crow (Corvus brachyrhynchos), family Corvidae.
Measurements printed on the plate exactly match Audubon: 16–21 in (41–53 cm), wingspan 34–39 in (86–99 cm), weight 11–22 oz (311–623 g).

The illustration matches the supplied reference photos and verified field marks: all-black glossy plumage with subtle blue-violet sheen, robust black bill, dark brown-black eye, black/slate legs and strong feet, broad wing, and short square-to-slightly-rounded tail rather than a raven-like wedge.
Behavior, habitat, social-family, communal-roost, ground-foraging, and methodical-flight claims are supported by the live Cornell and Audubon pages.
No material factual, text, species, or anatomical errors found. The plate shows one head, one beak, two wings, two legs, and one tail; no extra or missing parts are visible.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.
